title: CanadaLogin Metrics Cookbook
text:
Introduction
This cookbook documents commonly used CanadaLogin metrics, including their definitions, plain-language alternate names, calculation logic, and example SQL queries.
The cookbook is organized by data source:
Each metric generally includes:
The data source for each group of metrics is noted at the start of its section.
The goal is to make metric definitions easier to reuse, explain, and validate across reporting products.
The companion Data Catalog describes the underlying tables that these metrics are calculated from, field by field.

title: CanadaLogin Metrics Cookbook
text:
How to use this cookbook
Use this cookbook as a reference when building dashboards, reports, briefing materials, or ad hoc analyses.
For each metric, confirm:

Does it measure what I think it does?
What table and columns are used?
Should I apply any filters?
Is this metric a count, a rate, a cumulative value, or an average?

title: CanadaLogin Metrics Cookbook
text:
Conventions
All example SQL targets Amazon Athena (Trino dialect), since the tables live in the AWS Glue Data Catalog. Constructs such as DATE '...' literals, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (...), and REGEXP_REPLACE follow Trino semantics.
In reporting language, prefer the noun sign-in over login where possible. The underlying IBM Verify field may be called total_logins, but “sign-ins” is preferred (and often enforced) when talking about CanadaLogin.
Where applicable, internal applications are excluded from external-facing adoption reporting.
As of June 3, 2026, the current internal application exclusion list is:

Flow Application
GCS Migration Solution
GC Sign In - Profile Management App

title: CanadaLogin Metrics Cookbook
text:
Scope
This is a preliminary metrics cookbook to help with ad hoc CanadaLogin analyses. It generally deals with calculated metrics. Metrics that are already present in a table and don’t require any calculation do not need to be included, although they can be if they need clarification.
The initial version focuses on:

IBM Verify application sign-in metrics
call centre activity metrics
call centre topic metrics

Simple cross-source interpretive metrics comparing sign-in activity and call volume are planned for a future revision.
Additional data sources, metric definitions, and validation notes can be added as the cookbook matures.

title: IBM Verify
text:
CanadaLogin Metrics
Metrics in this section are calculated from IBM Verify authentication and application sign-in data.
The relevant IBM Verify tables are:
Internal applications. The following applications are typically excluded from public-facing adoption metrics:
Metrics in this section describe CanadaLogin overall, across all applications, sourced from ibm_verify.auth_total_logins.

title: IBM Verify
text:
Sign-Ins
The total number of sign-in events to CanadaLogin, including both successful and failed attempts.
Alternative names:

total sign-in attempts
sign-ins to CanadaLogin in a reporting period
total sign-ins to CanadaLogin

Calculation. Sum failed_logins and successful_logins over the selected reporting period.

SQLR


SELECT
    SUM(failed_logins + successful_logins) AS total_sign_in_attempts
FROM ibm_verify.auth_total_logins
-- select an arbitrary reporting period
WHERE from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-28';


tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "auth_total_logins")) |>
  # select an arbitrary reporting period
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-05-28")
  ) |>
  summarise(
    total_sign_in_attempts = sum(failed_logins + successful_logins, na.rm = TRUE)
  )





Sign-In Success Rate
The proportion of sign-in attempts that succeeded, across all of CanadaLogin.
This metric is useful for monitoring overall authentication health. A sustained drop can indicate a service problem, a misconfigured application, or elevated attack traffic inflating the failure count.
Alternative names:

authentication success rate
login success rate
overall sign-in success rate
successful sign-in proportion

Calculation. Divide the sum of successful_logins by the sum of all attempts (successful_logins + failed_logins) over the selected reporting period.

SQLR


SELECT
    CAST(SUM(successful_logins) AS DOUBLE)
        / NULLIF(SUM(successful_logins + failed_logins), 0) AS sign_in_success_rate
FROM ibm_verify.auth_total_logins
-- select an arbitrary reporting period
WHERE from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-28';


tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "auth_total_logins")) |>
  # select an arbitrary reporting period
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-05-28")
  ) |>
  summarise(
    sign_in_success_rate =
      sum(as.double(successful_logins), na.rm = TRUE) /
        sum(successful_logins + failed_logins, na.rm = TRUE)
  )



Interpretation notes. The rate is a proportion between 0 and 1. Read it alongside sign-in volume: a low success rate on very low volume may just be noise, while the same rate on high volume is a real signal.


Year-to-Date Unique Users
The total number of unique users who have signed in to CanadaLogin since the beginning of the current calendar year.
This metric is useful for understanding the overall reach and adoption of CanadaLogin over time. Note that this metric will not align with the sum of unique users across individual apps: a user who signs into App A and App B on the same day counts as two unique users at the app level but only one overall.
Alternative names:

total overall unique users this year
overall unique users YTD
year-to-date unique users of CanadaLogin

Calculation. Take the latest value of ytd_unique_users.

SQLR


SELECT
    ytd_unique_users
FROM ibm_verify.auth_total_logins
ORDER BY from_date DESC
LIMIT 1;


tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "auth_total_logins")) |>
  slice_max(
    from_date,
    n = 1,
    with_ties = FALSE
  ) |>
  pull(ytd_unique_users)

title: IBM Verify
text:
Application Metrics
Metrics in this section describe sign-in activity broken down by application, sourced from ibm_verify.app_login_counts.

Sign-Ins by Application
The total number of sign-ins recorded for each application over a selected time period.
This metric is useful for understanding overall usage volume across CanadaLogin-integrated applications. It counts sign-in events, not people. A single user may sign in multiple times and contribute multiple sign-ins.
This metric is referred to in the source data as total_logins, but in reporting language it is usually clearer to describe it as total sign-ins.
Alternative names:

total sign-ins
sign-in volume
number of sign-ins
application sign-in count
total uses of CanadaLogin by application

Calculation. Sum total_logins over the selected reporting period, grouped by application.

SQLR


SELECT
  application_name,
  SUM(total_logins) AS total_sign_ins
FROM   ibm_verify.app_login_counts
-- select an arbitrary reporting period
WHERE  from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-28'
-- optionally filter out internal applications
  AND application_name NOT IN (
      'Flow Application',
      'GCS Migration Solution',
      'GC Sign In - Profile Management App'
  )
GROUP  BY application_name
ORDER  BY total_sign_ins DESC;


internal_apps <- c(
  "Flow Application",
  "GCS Migration Solution",
  "GC Sign In - Profile Management App"
)

tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "app_login_counts")) |>
  # select an arbitrary reporting period
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-05-28"),
    # optionally filter out internal applications
    !application_name %in% internal_apps
  ) |>
  group_by(application_name) |>
  summarise(
    total_sign_ins = sum(total_logins, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(total_sign_ins))



Interpretation notes. A sign-in count measures events, not people. Use this metric when the question is about system usage or activity volume. Use unique user metrics when the question is about reach.


Sign-In Success Rate by Application
The proportion of sign-in attempts that succeeded, broken down by application.
This metric helps isolate authentication problems to a specific application. An application with a markedly lower success rate than its peers is worth investigating for integration or configuration issues.
Alternative names:

authentication success rate by application
login success rate by application
per-application sign-in success rate

Calculation. Divide the sum of successful_logins by the sum of all attempts (successful_logins + failed_logins) over the selected reporting period, grouped by application.

SQLR


SELECT
    application_name,
    CAST(SUM(successful_logins) AS DOUBLE)
        / NULLIF(SUM(successful_logins + failed_logins), 0) AS sign_in_success_rate
FROM ibm_verify.app_login_counts
-- select an arbitrary reporting period
WHERE from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-28'
-- optionally filter out internal applications
  AND application_name NOT IN (
      'Flow Application',
      'GCS Migration Solution',
      'GC Sign In - Profile Management App'
  )
GROUP BY application_name
ORDER BY sign_in_success_rate DESC;


internal_apps <- c(
  "Flow Application",
  "GCS Migration Solution",
  "GC Sign In - Profile Management App"
)

tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "app_login_counts")) |>
  # select an arbitrary reporting period
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-05-28"),
    # optionally filter out internal applications
    !application_name %in% internal_apps
  ) |>
  group_by(application_name) |>
  summarise(
    sign_in_success_rate =
      sum(as.double(successful_logins), na.rm = TRUE) /
        sum(successful_logins + failed_logins, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(sign_in_success_rate))



Interpretation notes. Compare applications against each other rather than against an absolute target. Note that this measures authentication against a relying party after already signing-in to CanadaLogin. It doesn’t requiring entering a password or two-factor, it simply checks if a given credential is valid for signing in to an app.


Peak Daily Unique Users
The highest number of unique users observed on any single day during the reporting period.
This metric is useful for identifying the maximum daily reach of an application and for understanding peak demand.
Alternative names:

peak daily users
highest daily unique users
maximum daily active users
busiest day by unique users
peak daily reach

Calculation. Take the maximum value of unique_users over the selected reporting period, grouped by application.

SQLR


WITH ranked AS (
    SELECT
        application_name,
        from_date,
        unique_users,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
            PARTITION BY application_name
            ORDER BY unique_users DESC, from_date ASC
        ) AS rn
    FROM ibm_verify.app_login_counts
    -- calculate over an arbitrary reporting period
    WHERE from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-28'
)

SELECT
    application_name,
    from_date,
    unique_users AS peak_daily_unique_users
FROM ranked
WHERE rn = 1
ORDER BY peak_daily_unique_users DESC;


tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "app_login_counts")) |>
  # calculate over an arbitrary reporting period
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-05-28")
  ) |>
  group_by(application_name) |>
  slice_max(
    unique_users,
    n = 1,
    with_ties = FALSE
  ) |>
  select(
    application_name,
    from_date,
    peak_daily_unique_users = unique_users
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(peak_daily_unique_users))



Interpretation notes. This metric identifies the busiest single day by unique user reach. The query above also returns the date on which that peak occurred.


Year-to-Date Unique Users
The number of unique users observed year-to-date for each application.
ytd_unique_users is a cumulative field that increases or remains stable over time. This metric is closer to “how many distinct people have used the application this year” than the daily unique user metrics.
Alternative names:

YTD unique users
unique users this year
year-to-date user reach
distinct users this year
total unique people reached this year

Calculation. Take the latest value of ytd_unique_users over the selected reporting period, grouped by application.

SQLR


WITH ranked AS (
    SELECT
        application_name,
        from_date,
        ytd_unique_users,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
            PARTITION BY application_name
            ORDER BY from_date DESC
        ) AS rn
    FROM ibm_verify.app_login_counts
    WHERE from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-28'
)

SELECT
    application_name,
    ytd_unique_users
FROM ranked
WHERE rn = 1
ORDER BY ytd_unique_users DESC;


tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "app_login_counts")) |>
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-05-28")
  ) |>
  group_by(application_name) |>
  slice_max(
    from_date,
    n = 1,
    with_ties = FALSE
  ) |>
  select(
    application_name,
    ytd_unique_users
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(ytd_unique_users))



Interpretation notes. Since ytd_unique_users is a cumulative field, it will reset every year.


Applications per Credential
The average number of distinct applications a single credential signs in to.
This metric measures how far the typical user spreads across CanadaLogin-integrated applications. It works because per-application unique_users counts each person once per application, while the CanadaLogin-wide count counts each person once overall: a user active on three applications adds three to the per-application sum but only one to the overall total. Dividing the two recovers the average number of applications per credential.
A value of 1.0 means the typical credential touches exactly one application; 2.0 means two, and so on.
Alternative names:

average applications per user
application breadth per credential
multi-app usage
cross-application reach
apps per account

Calculation. Sum the latest year-to-date unique users across every application (from app_login_counts), then divide by the latest CanadaLogin-wide year-to-date unique users (from auth_total_logins). Both inputs are cumulative ytd_unique_users fields, so take the latest value per source rather than summing across days.

SQLR


WITH app_latest AS (
    SELECT
        application_name,
        ytd_unique_users,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
            PARTITION BY application_name
            ORDER BY from_date DESC
        ) AS rn
    FROM ibm_verify.app_login_counts
),

app_total AS (
    SELECT SUM(ytd_unique_users) AS sum_app_unique_users
    FROM app_latest
    WHERE rn = 1
),

overall_total AS (
    SELECT ytd_unique_users AS overall_unique_users
    FROM ibm_verify.auth_total_logins
    ORDER BY from_date DESC
    LIMIT 1
)

SELECT
    CAST(app_total.sum_app_unique_users AS DOUBLE)
        / NULLIF(overall_total.overall_unique_users, 0) AS applications_per_credential
FROM app_total
CROSS JOIN overall_total;


sum_app_unique_users <- tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "app_login_counts")) |>
  group_by(application_name) |>
  slice_max(
    from_date,
    n = 1,
    with_ties = FALSE
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  summarise(
    sum_app_unique_users = sum(as.double(ytd_unique_users), na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  pull(sum_app_unique_users)

overall_unique_users <- tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "auth_total_logins")) |>
  slice_max(
    from_date,
    n = 1,
    with_ties = FALSE
  ) |>
  pull(ytd_unique_users)

sum_app_unique_users / overall_unique_users



Interpretation notes. This is a population average, not a per-user count: a small group of power users spanning many applications can pull it up while most credentials stay on one.
The figure is inflated by internal applications, especially the migration app (GCS Migration Solution) and the profile management app (GC Sign In - Profile Management App). A user signing in to a single public service is still recorded as a unique user in the migration app when their credential is migrated from ECM, and in the profile management app when they manage their account, so they count two or three times in the per-application sum while counting once overall. To measure breadth across public-facing services, exclude the internal applications (listed at the top of this chapter) from the per-application sum.
Swap ytd_unique_users for mtd_unique_users or since_apr22_unique_users to measure the same breadth over a different window.


Average Daily Sign-Ins
The average number of sign-ins per day for a specific application over a selected reporting period.
This metric summarizes daily sign-in activity into a single average value. It is useful for understanding typical daily usage during a launch period, reporting window, or monitoring period.
Alternative names:

average sign-ins per day
typical daily sign-in volume
mean daily login volume
average daily CanadaLogin activity

Calculation. For each application, calculate the average of total_logins across all daily records in the selected reporting period.

SQLR


SELECT
    application_name,
    AVG(total_logins) AS average_daily_sign_ins
FROM ibm_verify.app_login_counts
-- choose an arbitrary reporting period
WHERE from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-04-22' AND DATE '2026-05-31'
GROUP BY application_name
ORDER BY average_daily_sign_ins DESC;


tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "app_login_counts")) |>
  # choose an arbitrary reporting period
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-04-22"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-05-31")
  ) |>
  group_by(application_name) |>
  summarise(
    average_daily_sign_ins = mean(total_logins, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(average_daily_sign_ins))

title: IBM Verify
text:
Multi-factor Authentication Metrics
Metrics in this section describe multi-factor authentication (MFA) activity, sourced from ibm_verify.mfa_activity. That table has one row per day per MFA factor (mfa_type) per outcome (result), with the number of attempts in count.

MFA Authentications
The total number of MFA attempts over a selected reporting period.
This metric measures second-factor authentication volume. It counts attempts, not people, and includes both successful and failed challenges.
Alternative names:

MFA attempts
two-factor authentications
total MFA challenges
MFA volume

Calculation. Sum count over the selected reporting period. Add a result breakdown to separate successes from failures.

SQLR


SELECT
    SUM(count) AS mfa_authentications
FROM ibm_verify.mfa_activity
-- select an arbitrary reporting period
WHERE from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-28';


tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "mfa_activity")) |>
  # select an arbitrary reporting period
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-05-28")
  ) |>
  summarise(
    mfa_authentications = sum(count, na.rm = TRUE)
  )





MFA Success Rate
The proportion of MFA attempts that succeeded over a selected reporting period.
This metric is useful for monitoring how often users clear the second factor. A falling rate can point to user friction with a particular factor, delivery problems (for example, undelivered SMS or email OTPs), or attack traffic.
Alternative names:

MFA pass rate
second-factor success rate
MFA completion rate

Calculation. Divide the sum of count where result = 'success' by the sum of count across all results.

SQLR


SELECT
    CAST(SUM(count) FILTER (WHERE result = 'success') AS DOUBLE)
        / NULLIF(SUM(count), 0) AS mfa_success_rate
FROM ibm_verify.mfa_activity
-- select an arbitrary reporting period
WHERE from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-28';


tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "mfa_activity")) |>
  # select an arbitrary reporting period
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-05-28")
  ) |>
  summarise(
    mfa_success_rate =
      sum(if_else(result == "success", as.double(count), 0), na.rm = TRUE) /
        sum(count, na.rm = TRUE)
  )



Interpretation notes. The rate is a proportion between 0 and 1. Interpret per factor where possible: a low overall rate may be driven by a single struggling factor rather than a system-wide problem.


MFA Activity by Factor
The volume of MFA attempts broken down by factor type, with each factor’s share of total activity.
This metric shows which second factors users actually rely on. In the current data the mix is dominated by sms_otp and email_otp, with factors such as fido2, totp, kq, qr, and ibm_verify_push seeing little or no use.
Alternative names:

MFA factor mix
second-factor distribution
MFA usage by method
MFA factor share

Calculation. Sum count grouped by mfa_type, then divide each factor’s total by the overall total to get its share.

SQLR


SELECT
    mfa_type,
    SUM(count) AS mfa_authentications,
    CAST(SUM(count) AS DOUBLE)
        / NULLIF(SUM(SUM(count)) OVER (), 0) AS factor_share
FROM ibm_verify.mfa_activity
-- select an arbitrary reporting period
WHERE from_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-28'
GROUP BY mfa_type
ORDER BY mfa_authentications DESC;


tbl(con, in_schema("ibm_verify", "mfa_activity")) |>
  # select an arbitrary reporting period
  filter(
    from_date >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    from_date <= as.Date("2026-05-28")
  ) |>
  group_by(mfa_type) |>
  summarise(
    mfa_authentications = sum(count, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  mutate(
    factor_share = as.double(mfa_authentications) / sum(mfa_authentications)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(mfa_authentications))



Interpretation notes. factor_share is a proportion between 0 and 1 and sums to 1 across all factors. Combine with the MFA success rate to spot factors that are both heavily used and failing often.

title: Call Centre
text:
Activity Metrics
Metrics in this section are calculated from call centre activity and topic data.
Call centre metrics are useful for understanding support demand, service responsiveness, caller wait times, average call duration, and common reasons for contact. The source data is weekly, so these metrics are typically reported at a weekly level or aggregated across weeks.
The relevant call centre tables are:
date_range_start and date_range_end are stored as text, so date-range filters cast them to DATE before comparing to date literals.
Metrics in this section describe call centre operations, sourced from call_centre.weekly_activity_report.

title: Call Centre
text:
Calls Accepted
The number of calls accepted by the call centre during a reporting period.
This metric is useful for tracking call centre demand and understanding how contact volume changes over time.
Alternative names:

calls received
accepted calls
weekly call volume
call centre demand
number of calls handled by the queue

Calculation. Use calls_accepted by reporting period, or sum across periods for a total.

SQLR


By week:
SELECT
    date_range_start,
    date_range_end,
    CAST(calls_accepted AS BIGINT) AS calls_accepted
FROM call_centre.weekly_activity_report
ORDER BY date_range_start;
Total over a period:
SELECT
    SUM(CAST(calls_accepted AS BIGINT)) AS total_calls_accepted
FROM call_centre.weekly_activity_report
WHERE CAST(date_range_start AS DATE) BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-31';


By week:
tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_activity_report")) |>
  arrange(date_range_start) |>
  select(
    date_range_start,
    date_range_end,
    calls_accepted
  )
Total over a period:
tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_activity_report")) |>
  filter(
    as.Date(date_range_start) >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    as.Date(date_range_start) <= as.Date("2026-05-31")
  ) |>
  summarise(
    total_calls_accepted = sum(calls_accepted, na.rm = TRUE)
  )



Interpretation notes. Calls accepted may not be identical to calls answered, depending on call centre definitions and routing logic.


Calls Answered
The number of calls answered by call centre agents during a reporting period.
This metric is also used as a weighting variable when calculating weighted average call length and weighted average delay.
Alternative names:

answered calls
calls handled
agent-answered calls
completed calls
calls taken by agents

Calculation. Use calls_answered by reporting period, or sum across periods for a total.

SQLR


By week:
SELECT
    date_range_start,
    date_range_end,
    CAST(calls_answered AS BIGINT) AS calls_answered
FROM call_centre.weekly_activity_report
ORDER BY date_range_start;
Total over a period:
SELECT
    SUM(CAST(calls_answered AS BIGINT)) AS total_calls_answered
FROM call_centre.weekly_activity_report
WHERE CAST(date_range_start AS DATE) BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-31';


By week:
tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_activity_report")) |>
  arrange(date_range_start) |>
  select(
    date_range_start,
    date_range_end,
    calls_answered
  )
Total over a period:
tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_activity_report")) |>
  filter(
    as.Date(date_range_start) >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    as.Date(date_range_start) <= as.Date("2026-05-31")
  ) |>
  summarise(
    total_calls_answered = sum(calls_answered, na.rm = TRUE)
  )



Interpretation notes. Calls answered is commonly used as the weighting field when combining weekly average call length or delay values.


Average Call Length
The average length of answered calls.
The overall average call length is calculated as a weighted mean of avg_call_length_seconds, weighted by calls_answered. This is preferable to a simple average of weekly averages because weeks with more answered calls should contribute more to the overall result.
Alternative names:

average call duration
average handle time
average length of call
typical call length
average time on call

Calculation. Calculate a weighted average of weekly average call length, weighted by the number of answered calls.

SQLR


Weighted average over a period:
SELECT
    SUM(
        CAST(avg_call_length_seconds AS DOUBLE)
        * CAST(calls_answered AS DOUBLE)
    )
    / NULLIF(SUM(CAST(calls_answered AS DOUBLE)), 0) AS weighted_avg_call_length_seconds
FROM call_centre.weekly_activity_report
WHERE CAST(date_range_start AS DATE) BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-31';
By week:
SELECT
    date_range_start,
    date_range_end,
    CAST(avg_call_length_seconds AS DOUBLE) AS avg_call_length_seconds,
    CAST(calls_answered AS BIGINT) AS calls_answered
FROM call_centre.weekly_activity_report
ORDER BY date_range_start;


Weighted average over a period:
tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_activity_report")) |>
  filter(
    as.Date(date_range_start) >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    as.Date(date_range_start) <= as.Date("2026-05-31")
  ) |>
  summarise(
    weighted_avg_call_length_seconds =
      sum(avg_call_length_seconds * calls_answered, na.rm = TRUE) /
        sum(calls_answered, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
By week:
tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_activity_report")) |>
  arrange(date_range_start) |>
  select(
    date_range_start,
    date_range_end,
    avg_call_length_seconds,
    calls_answered
  )



Interpretation notes. Use a weighted average rather than a simple average of weekly averages. A simple average may give too much influence to low-volume weeks.


Average Delay
The average delay before calls are answered.
This metric is useful for understanding caller wait times and call centre responsiveness. Like average call length, it should be calculated as a weighted mean to avoid low-volume weeks distorting the result.
Alternative names:

average wait time
average delay before answer
caller wait time
time to answer
average queue delay

Calculation. Calculate a weighted average of weekly average delay, weighted by the number of answered calls.

SQLR


Weighted average over a period:
SELECT
    SUM(
        CAST(avg_delay_seconds AS DOUBLE)
        * CAST(calls_answered AS DOUBLE)
    )
    / NULLIF(SUM(CAST(calls_answered AS DOUBLE)), 0) AS weighted_avg_delay_seconds
FROM call_centre.weekly_activity_report
WHERE CAST(date_range_start AS DATE) BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-31';
By week:
SELECT
    date_range_start,
    date_range_end,
    CAST(avg_delay_seconds AS DOUBLE) AS avg_delay_seconds,
    CAST(calls_answered AS BIGINT) AS calls_answered
FROM call_centre.weekly_activity_report
ORDER BY date_range_start;


Weighted average over a period:
tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_activity_report")) |>
  filter(
    as.Date(date_range_start) >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    as.Date(date_range_start) <= as.Date("2026-05-31")
  ) |>
  summarise(
    weighted_avg_delay_seconds =
      sum(avg_delay_seconds * calls_answered, na.rm = TRUE) /
        sum(calls_answered, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
By week:
tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_activity_report")) |>
  arrange(date_range_start) |>
  select(
    date_range_start,
    date_range_end,
    avg_delay_seconds,
    calls_answered
  )



Interpretation notes. Average delay should be interpreted alongside call volume. A low-volume week can produce unusual wait times that should not dominate the overall result.


Abandonment Rate
The proportion of accepted calls that were abandoned before being answered.
This metric is useful for understanding how often callers give up while waiting. It is closely tied to wait times: rising delay often drives rising abandonment.
Alternative names:

abandon rate
call abandonment rate
drop rate
proportion of calls abandoned

Calculation. Divide the sum of calls_abandoned by the sum of calls_accepted over the selected reporting period. Summing the raw counts gives a volume-weighted rate; do not average the weekly calls_abandoned_pct column.

SQLR


SELECT
    SUM(CAST(calls_abandoned AS DOUBLE))
        / NULLIF(SUM(CAST(calls_accepted AS DOUBLE)), 0) AS abandonment_rate
FROM call_centre.weekly_activity_report
WHERE CAST(date_range_start AS DATE) BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-31';


tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_activity_report")) |>
  filter(
    as.Date(date_range_start) >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    as.Date(date_range_start) <= as.Date("2026-05-31")
  ) |>
  summarise(
    abandonment_rate =
      sum(calls_abandoned, na.rm = TRUE) /
        sum(calls_accepted, na.rm = TRUE)
  )



Interpretation notes. The rate is a proportion between 0 and 1. Read it together with average delay; a spike in abandonment usually coincides with longer waits.


Answer Rate
The proportion of accepted calls that were answered by an agent.
This metric is the complement of abandonment and any other non-answered outcomes. It summarizes how much of incoming demand the call centre actually served.
Alternative names:

answer ratio
proportion of calls answered
pickup rate
calls answered rate

Calculation. Divide the sum of calls_answered by the sum of calls_accepted over the selected reporting period.

SQLR


SELECT
    SUM(CAST(calls_answered AS DOUBLE))
        / NULLIF(SUM(CAST(calls_accepted AS DOUBLE)), 0) AS answer_rate
FROM call_centre.weekly_activity_report
WHERE CAST(date_range_start AS DATE) BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-31';


tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_activity_report")) |>
  filter(
    as.Date(date_range_start) >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    as.Date(date_range_start) <= as.Date("2026-05-31")
  ) |>
  summarise(
    answer_rate =
      sum(calls_answered, na.rm = TRUE) /
        sum(calls_accepted, na.rm = TRUE)
  )



Interpretation notes. The rate is a proportion between 0 and 1. A high answer rate with rising delay can still indicate strain: calls are answered eventually, but callers wait longer to reach an agent.


Service Level (Answered Within 60s)
The proportion of calls answered within 60 seconds.
This metric is a standard call centre service-level measure of how quickly the queue is cleared. The source data stores only the weekly proportion (pct_answered_within_60s), not the underlying within-60s count, so combine weeks with a weighted average rather than re-deriving from counts.
Alternative names:

service level
SLA
grade of service
percent answered within 60 seconds
speed of answer

Calculation. Calculate a weighted average of pct_answered_within_60s, weighted by calls_answered.

SQLR


SELECT
    SUM(
        CAST(pct_answered_within_60s AS DOUBLE)
        * CAST(calls_answered AS DOUBLE)
    )
    / NULLIF(SUM(CAST(calls_answered AS DOUBLE)), 0) AS service_level_60s
FROM call_centre.weekly_activity_report
WHERE CAST(date_range_start AS DATE) BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-31';


tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_activity_report")) |>
  filter(
    as.Date(date_range_start) >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    as.Date(date_range_start) <= as.Date("2026-05-31")
  ) |>
  summarise(
    service_level_60s =
      sum(pct_answered_within_60s * calls_answered, na.rm = TRUE) /
        sum(calls_answered, na.rm = TRUE)
  )



Interpretation notes. The result is a proportion between 0 and 1. Because only the weekly proportion is stored, the weighted average is an approximation of the true within-60s rate, but it is far closer than a simple average of weekly values.


Average Handle Time
The average total time agents spend per call, including any after-call wrap-up.
avg_handle_time_seconds covers more than avg_call_length_seconds: it adds wrap-up work after the caller disconnects. Like the other averages, combine weeks with a weighted mean.
Alternative names:

AHT
average handling time
total handle time per call
average talk plus wrap time

Calculation. Calculate a weighted average of avg_handle_time_seconds, weighted by calls_answered.

SQLR


SELECT
    SUM(
        CAST(avg_handle_time_seconds AS DOUBLE)
        * CAST(calls_answered AS DOUBLE)
    )
    / NULLIF(SUM(CAST(calls_answered AS DOUBLE)), 0) AS weighted_avg_handle_time_seconds
FROM call_centre.weekly_activity_report
WHERE CAST(date_range_start AS DATE) BETWEEN DATE '2026-01-01' AND DATE '2026-05-31';


tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_activity_report")) |>
  filter(
    as.Date(date_range_start) >= as.Date("2026-01-01"),
    as.Date(date_range_start) <= as.Date("2026-05-31")
  ) |>
  summarise(
    weighted_avg_handle_time_seconds =
      sum(avg_handle_time_seconds * calls_answered, na.rm = TRUE) /
        sum(calls_answered, na.rm = TRUE)
  )



Interpretation notes. Average handle time is always at least as large as average call length. A widening gap between the two points to growing after-call work.

title: Call Centre
text:
Topic Metrics
Metrics in this section describe why people contact the call centre, sourced from call_centre.weekly_topic_dump. Because a single call can generate multiple topic rows, these metrics count topic records rather than distinct calls. Clean statobject before grouping, since it often carries leading, trailing, or repeated whitespace.

Call Topic Share
The percentage of all topic records represented by each call topic.
This is useful for understanding the relative importance of each call driver compared to the overall volume.
Alternative names:

share of calls by topic
percentage of calls by reason
topic distribution
call reason mix
contact driver share

Calculation. Count records by cleaned topic, then divide each topic count by the total count across all topics.

SQLR


WITH topic_counts AS (
    SELECT
        REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(statobject), '\s+', ' ') AS call_topic,
        COUNT(*) AS topic_count
    FROM call_centre.weekly_topic_dump
    GROUP BY REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(statobject), '\s+', ' ')
)

SELECT
    call_topic,
    topic_count,
    CAST(topic_count AS DOUBLE)
        / NULLIF(SUM(topic_count) OVER (), 0) AS topic_share
FROM topic_counts
ORDER BY topic_count DESC;


tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_topic_dump")) |>
  mutate(
    call_topic = str_squish(statobject)
  ) |>
  count(call_topic, name = "topic_count") |>
  mutate(
    topic_share = as.double(topic_count) / sum(topic_count)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(topic_count))



Interpretation notes. Topic share is useful for comparing relative demand across contact reasons. Interpret alongside total call volume.


Top 10 Call Topics
The ten most common call topics in the call centre topic data.
This is useful for quickly identifying the most common reasons people contact the call centre.
Alternative names:

top 10 call reasons
most common call topics
top contact drivers
main reasons for calling
top caller issues

Calculation. Count records by cleaned topic, calculate topic share, and return the top 10 by topic count.

SQLR


WITH topic_counts AS (
    SELECT
        REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(statobject), '\s+', ' ') AS call_topic,
        COUNT(*) AS topic_count
    FROM call_centre.weekly_topic_dump
    GROUP BY REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(statobject), '\s+', ' ')
),

topic_shares AS (
    SELECT
        call_topic,
        topic_count,
        CAST(topic_count AS DOUBLE)
            / NULLIF(SUM(topic_count) OVER (), 0) AS topic_share
    FROM topic_counts
)

SELECT
    call_topic,
    topic_count,
    topic_share
FROM topic_shares
ORDER BY topic_count DESC
LIMIT 10;


tbl(con, in_schema("call_centre", "weekly_topic_dump")) |>
  mutate(
    call_topic = str_squish(statobject)
  ) |>
  count(call_topic, name = "topic_count") |>
  mutate(
    topic_share = as.double(topic_count) / sum(topic_count)
  ) |>
  slice_max(
    topic_count,
    n = 10,
    with_ties = FALSE
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(topic_count))



Interpretation notes. The top 10 view is useful for quick reporting but may hide important lower-volume issues. In cases where there are not many calls, it may be useful to limit this list to a top 5, or to truncate ties at the end of the list.

title: Notes on Metric Interpretation
text:
Sign-ins are not users
A sign-in count measures events. One person can sign in multiple times.
Use sign-ins when the question is about volume or system usage.
Use unique users when the question is about reach.

title: Notes on Metric Interpretation
section: Daily unique users are not the same as period unique users
text:
Daily unique users are not the same as period unique users
Summing daily unique users gives a measure of user-days, not distinct people over the full period.
For distinct people over a longer period, use a cumulative unique user field such as ytd_unique_users or since_apr22_unique_users, if available and valid.

title: Notes on Metric Interpretation
section: Weighted averages should be used for call centre averages
text:
Weighted averages should be used for call centre averages
When calculating average call length or average delay across multiple weeks, use a weighted average based on calls_answered.
A simple average of weekly averages may give too much influence to low-volume weeks.

title: Notes on Metric Interpretation
section: Internal applications should usually be excluded from external adoption reporting
text:
Internal applications should usually be excluded from external adoption reporting
The following applications are currently treated as internal:

Flow Application
GCS Migration Solution
GC Sign In - Profile Management App

Whether to exclude them depends on the reporting question.

title: About
text:
IBM Verify
The data catalog describes the tables available to analysts. It is distinct from the metrics cookbook; the cookbook explains how to derive metrics, while the catalog describes the tables those metrics are calculated from, field by field.
Derived tables in the ibm_verify schema, pulled from the IBM Verify reporting API.

title: About
text:
app_login_counts - daily sign-in counts by application
auth_total_logins - daily, CanadaLogin-wide sign-in totals
mfa_activity - multi-factor authentication activity by factor and result

title: About
text:
Call Centre
Weekly call centre data extracted from the CanadaLogin support weekly Excel reports.

weekly_activity_report - weekly call volume and service-level summary
weekly_topic_dump - individual call records with topic classifications

title: About
text:
Conventions
Field types use Parquet-style names (boolean, date, float, integer, string, time, timestamp). For string columns that behave as factors, the possible values are listed. Cumulative fields (those that only increase within a window and then reset) are called out explicitly. Proportions are stored as values between 0 and 1, not 0 and 100.

title: IBM Verify
text:
app_login_counts
Derived tables in the ibm_verify schema, populated from the IBM Verify reporting API. Each table is a daily aggregate derived from the raw event stream in ibm_verify_raw.ibm_verify.
Daily sign-in counts for each CanadaLogin-integrated application.
Each row represents one application on one day, summarizing that application’s sign-in activity and unique-user reach for the day.
Keywords: sign-ins, logins, applications, unique users, adoption, daily counts

title: IBM Verify
text:
Provenance
Queried directly from the IBM Verify production instance via API.

Updated: Daily, early in the morning
Steward: EDCP Data and Research Team
Contact: Yinlin Zhang

title: IBM Verify
text:
Fields
One row per application_name per day.








Field Name
Type
Description




application_name
string
Name of the integrated application (for example, “IRIS - PRC - Prod”)


from_date
timestamp
Start of the reporting day (midnight)


to_date
timestamp
End of the reporting day (23:59:59)


total_logins
integer
Total sign-in events recorded for the application that day. Counts events, not people. Reported as “total sign-ins”


unique_users
integer
Number of distinct users who signed in to the application that day


failed_logins
integer
Number of failed sign-in attempts that day


successful_logins
integer
Number of successful sign-ins that day


mtd_unique_users
integer
Cumulative distinct users for the application month-to-date. Resets at the start of each month


ytd_unique_users
integer
Cumulative distinct users for the application year-to-date. Resets at the start of each calendar year


since_apr22_unique_users
integer
Cumulative distinct users since the April 22, 2026 public launch


fetched_at
timestamp
When the aggregation last fetched this row


date
string
The reporting day as a text date (for example, “2026-04-23”). Mirrors from_date

title: IBM Verify
text:
Notes
total_logins, unique_users, failed_logins, and successful_logins are daily values. mtd_unique_users, ytd_unique_users, and since_apr22_unique_users are cumulative counts of distinct users and should not be summed across rows; take the latest value within the window instead.

title: IBM Verify
text:
Provenance
Queried directly from the IBM Verify production instance via API.

Updated: Daily, early in the morning
Steward: EDCP Data and Research Team
Contact: Yinlin Zhang

title: IBM Verify
text:
Fields
One row per day.








Field Name
Type
Description




from_date
timestamp
Start of the reporting day (midnight)


to_date
timestamp
End of the reporting day (23:59:59)


unique_users
integer
Number of distinct users who signed in to CanadaLogin that day


failed_logins
integer
Number of failed sign-in attempts that day


successful_logins
integer
Number of successful sign-ins that day


mtd_unique_users
integer
Cumulative distinct users month-to-date. Resets at the start of each month


ytd_unique_users
integer
Cumulative distinct users year-to-date. Resets at the start of each calendar year


since_apr22_unique_users
integer
Cumulative distinct users since the April 22, 2026 launch period


last_updated_at
timestamp
When the underlying data for this row was last updated


fetched_at
timestamp
When the aggregation last fetched this row


date
string
The reporting day as a text date (for example, “2026-05-21”). Mirrors from_date

title: IBM Verify
text:
Notes
unique_users, failed_logins, and successful_logins are daily values. The mtd_, ytd_, and since_apr22_ unique-user fields are cumulative and should not be summed across rows; take the latest value within the window instead.

title: IBM Verify
text:
Provenance
Queried directly from the IBM Verify production instance via API.

Updated: Daily, early in the morning
Steward: EDCP Data and Research Team
Contact: Yinlin Zhang

title: IBM Verify
text:
Fields
One row per day per mfa_type per result.








Field Name
Type
Description




from_date
timestamp
Start of the reporting day (midnight)


to_date
timestamp
End of the reporting day (23:59:59)


mfa_type
string
The MFA factor used. One of “sms_otp”, “fido2”, “kq”, “totp”, “voice_otp”, “qr”, “ibm_verify_push”, “email_otp”


result
string
Outcome of the MFA attempts. One of “success” or “failure”


count
integer
Number of MFA attempts for this day, factor, and result


fetched_at
timestamp
When the aggregation last fetched this row


date
string
The reporting day as a text date (for example, “2026-05-31”). Mirrors from_date

title: IBM Verify
text:
Notes
Because the grain is day x factor x result, totalling MFA activity for a day requires summing count across all mfa_type and result rows for that day. Filter on result = 'success' to count successful second-factor authentications.

title: Call Centre
text:
weekly_activity_report
Weekly call centre data extracted from the CanadaLogin support queue Excel reports. These two tables share the same source and update cadence but are at different grains: weekly_activity_report is one row per week, while weekly_topic_dump is one row per topic record per call.
Weekly summary of call centre operations for the CanadaLogin support queue.
Each row represents one reporting week, summarizing call volume, service levels, call outcomes, and call type breakdowns for that week.
Keywords: call centre, calls, weekly, volume, wait time, call length, service level

title: Call Centre
text:
Provenance
Sourced from a weekly Excel report that is typically sent to the EDCP Data and Research team via email. That spreadsheet is extracted and loaded into the data repository via the call centre weekly activity report Lambda. The extracted_at field records when each row was last extracted from the source file.

Updated: Weekly, typically on Tuesdays
Steward: EDCP Data and Research Team
Contact: Colin Douglas

title: Call Centre
text:
Fields
One row per reporting week.
Many count fields are stored as float because they originate from Excel. Proportions (fields ending in _pct) are stored as values between 0 and 1, not 0 and 100.








Field Name
Type
Description




week_text
string
Report title, extracted from the Excel file


date_range_start
string
Start date of the reporting week as a text date (for example, “2026-04-26”)


date_range_end
string
End date of the reporting week as a text date (for example, “2026-05-02”)


date
string
Equivalent to date_range_start; used as the primary date key


file
string
Source filename the row was extracted from


extracted_at
string
ISO 8601 timestamp of when the row was extracted from the source file


calls_accepted
float
Total calls accepted by the queue during the week


calls_accepted_pct
float
Proportion of calls accepted (typically 1.0)


calls_abandoned
float
Number of calls abandoned before being answered


calls_abandoned_pct
float
Proportion of accepted calls that were abandoned


calls_answered
float
Number of calls answered by an agent


pct_answered_within_60s
float
Proportion of calls answered within 60 seconds


avg_delay_seconds
float
Average wait time before a call was answered, in seconds


avg_call_length_seconds
float
Average duration of answered calls, in seconds


avg_handle_time_seconds
float
Average total handle time per call (call length plus any wrap-up time), in seconds


calls_completed_by_io
float
Number of calls completed by the information officer


calls_completed_by_io_pct
float
Proportion of answered calls completed by the information officer


hangups
float
Number of calls where the caller hung up


hangups_pct
float
Proportion of answered calls that ended in a hangup


test_calls
float
Number of test calls made during the week


test_calls_pct
float
Proportion of accepted calls that were test calls


total_calls_answered
float
Total calls answered (may differ from calls_answered depending on reporting logic)


total_calls_answered_pct
float
Proportion of accepted calls that were answered


comments
float
Number of calls where a comment was recorded


comments_pct
float
Proportion of answered calls with a comment


callback
float
Number of calls where a callback was requested or offered


callback_pct
float
Proportion of answered calls that involved a callback


no_information_provided
float
Number of calls where no information was provided to the caller


no_information_provided_pct
float
Proportion of answered calls where no information was provided


transfer_to_crisis_centres
float
Number of calls transferred to a crisis centre


transfer_to_crisis_centres_pct
float
Proportion of answered calls transferred to a crisis centre


program_content_provided
float
Number of calls where program content was provided to the caller


program_content_provided_pct
float
Proportion of answered calls where program content was provided


total_of_call_types
float
Total number of call-type records for the week (used as denominator for call-type proportions)


total_of_call_types_pct
float
Proportion of total call types (typically 1.0)

title: Call Centre
text:
Notes
avg_delay_seconds and avg_call_length_seconds are weekly averages sourced from the report directly. When combining across weeks, use a weighted average with calls_answered as the weight rather than a simple average. See the Call Centre metrics chapter for example SQL.
date_range_start and date_range_end are stored as strings. Cast to DATE in SQL when filtering by date range.

title: Call Centre
text:
Provenance
Sourced from a weekly Excel report that is typically sent to the EDCP Data and Research team via email. That spreadsheet is extracted and loaded into the data repository via the call_centre_etl.py Lambda. The extracted_at field records when each row was last extracted from the source file.

Updated: Weekly, typically on Tuesdays
Steward: EDCP Data and Research Team
Contact: Colin Douglas

title: Call Centre
text:
Fields
One row per topic record per call. A single call (call_no) may appear on multiple rows if more than one topic was recorded.








Field Name
Type
Description




call_no
integer
Identifier for the call. Not unique - multiple rows share the same call_no when multiple topics were recorded


call_datetime
integer
Call timestamp stored as a Unix nanosecond integer. Cast to timestamp before use


call_date
date
Date the call took place


date
string
Call date as a text date. Mirrors call_date


clienttype
string
Type of caller, for example “Business”, “Public”, “Government of Canada”


language
string
Language of the call, for example “English”, “French”


origin
string
Province or region the caller is calling from


statobject
string
The call topic as recorded in the source system. May contain leading/trailing whitespace or inconsistent spacing


cstat
string
Full topic classification path, including category and topic (for example, “Main Document description::What is CanadaLogin?”)


transactiontype
string
Type of transaction recorded for the topic, for example “Search”, “Callback”


programcontact
string
Program contact information provided to the caller, if any. Often missing


source
float
Unused

title: Call Centre
text:
Notes
Because a single call can generate multiple topic rows, do not count rows to get call volume. Use COUNT(DISTINCT call_no) or join to weekly_activity_report for call counts.
statobject frequently contains leading/trailing whitespace and repeated internal spaces. Always clean it before grouping:
REGEXP_REPLACE(TRIM(statobject), '\s+', ' ') AS call_topic
See the Call Centre metrics chapter for topic count and topic share example SQL.
call_datetime is a Unix timestamp in nanoseconds. Divide by 1,000,000,000 before converting to a timestamp:
from_unixtime(call_datetime / 1000000000) AS call_timestamp
